text.title: Support SLA — Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules for solo-SaaS support tiering, business-hours boundary, and canned-reply discipline.

rule.statement: Define EXACTLY three tiers: Community (free), Paid (≥ $19/mo), Enterprise (annual + signed contract). Sub-tiers within Paid (Solo / Pro / Geek) collapse into the Paid SLA — solos cannot operate 5 different SLAs.
rule.rationale: Operator cognitive load is the bottleneck, not customer-tier granularity. Three tiers cover > 95% of solo SaaS support patterns; more tiers create rule-lookup overhead that erodes reply time.

rule.statement: SLA reply times count business-hours only (Mon-Fri, operator timezone, declared start-end). Public SLA page must show timezone and the working window. After-hours = next business day.

rationale.source: Solo-founder burnout cohort (Justin Jackson, Sarah Hatter ChatTriage essays)

rule.rationale: "24h response" interpreted as wall-clock 24h drives weekend work and resentment. Business-hours framing is the only sustainable solo policy and most enterprise customers accept it explicitly.

rule.statement: Operator must use canned-reply skeletons for ≥ 60% of replies. Each canned reply has 3 slots: customer name, ticket reference, specific finding. Custom prose for < 40%.
rule.rationale: Canned replies cut median reply time 4-7×. Quality stays equal — customers care about resolution, not bespoke prose. The 60% threshold is the inflection point where operator gains time without sounding robotic.

rule.statement: Escalate to "drop everything" mode ONLY if: data loss reported, security incident reported, paying customer churned within 7 days, or 3+ users reporting the same break. All other tickets follow tier SLA.

rationale.source: Production-incident triage discipline (SRE Book, Beyer et al.)

rule.rationale: Without explicit escalation criteria, the loudest customer wins. Concrete triggers force objective triage and protect calendar from squeaky-wheel exploitation.

rule.statement: Every reply MUST tag the ticket with a churn-risk signal: GREEN (happy / praise), YELLOW (confusion / friction), RED (frustration / threatens to cancel). Tag drives weekly retention review, NOT the reply itself.

rationale.source: Customer success industry standard (Lincoln Murphy, Gainsight)

rule.rationale: Solo founders see every ticket in real time but lose the pattern over weeks. The tag is cheap to apply and the pattern (RED clusters) is the leading churn indicator.

example.title: Default SLA tiers (operator can adjust)
example.code:
Community (free): First reply 72h business hours, no weekend. Paid ($19+/mo): First reply 24h business hours, priority queue. Enterprise: First reply 4h business hours, dedicated channel (Slack or signal), monthly review.
Working hours: Mon-Fri 09:00-17:00 Europe/Lisbon (operator's TZ). Out-of-hours auto-reply: "Got it. Reply by next business day per your tier SLA. See SLA: faion.net/sla."
example.note: Concrete numbers + visible timezone = trust signal without burnout.

example.title: Wall-clock SLA without timezone (r2 violation)
example.code:
"We reply within 24 hours."
Customer in Tokyo sends ticket at midnight Lisbon. 24h elapses at midnight Lisbon next day. Operator wakes at 7am, already breached. Reputation damage from a metric the operator could not physically meet.
example.note: Violates r2. Always state timezone AND business-hours window.
